label: delphi-r1-p6
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from close analogies: how have adjacent or analogous situations been treated?
text: {"probability":68,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not that a reviewing court finds at least a substantial part of the Water Resilience Surcharge invalid under art. XIII D, § 6(b)(3), because the district's own rate study admits the $0.85 figure is derived \"in part\" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation (the core defect condemned in Capistrano), and because a majority of the revenue funds rebates that the district concedes mostly benefit non-payors — a cross-subsidy problem independent of the pricing defect. The district's fallback \"not a property-related fee\" argument is weak under Bighorn and unlikely to avoid merits review. Countervailing cost-justification (the 1.6x marginal-cost finding) and the conservation mandate/Water Code § 375 give the district real, non-frivolous defenses that could save the debt-service (30%) portion and possibly cap rather than eliminate the surcharge, which is why this isn't a near-certainty.","rationales":["Capistrano holds tiered rates must track the actual marginal cost of service at that tier, not conservation goals alone, and the district's concession that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation is close to the precise defect Capistrano invalidated.","The rate study's own 1.6x marginal-cost finding sets an internal benchmark against which the $0.85 surcharge can be tested, so any increment above a cost-justified differential is independently vulnerable even if the whole surcharge is not struck down.","Routing 55% of surcharge revenue to rebates that the district admits 'mostly benefit customers other than those paying the surcharge' creates a cross-subsidization theory distinct from the pricing theory, giving plaintiffs two independent paths to a 6(b)(3) violation and increasing the odds that at least 'a substantial part' falls.","The district's alternative argument that the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all is likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil, which treats usage-based charges to existing water customers as property-related fees, so the case will almost certainly be resolved on the cost-of-service merits rather than dismissed at the threshold.","Countervailing factors — a genuine 1.6x cost differential, Article X § 2's conservation mandate, Water Code § 375's express legislative endorsement of conservation pricing, and Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir's allowance of capital/debt-service cost recovery through volumetric rates (supporting the 30% recycled-water debt-service allocation) — give the district a real chance of preserving a meaningful part of the structure, which is why I place this below 75 rather than higher."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(3)","Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2","Water Code § 375","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372","Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) — recent appellate treatment of tiered-rate proportionality, holding unverified, flag for confirmation"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court will treat the conceded 'conservation-incentive' pricing component and the rebate cross-subsidy as severable, partial defects (satisfying 'substantial part' invalidation while preserving the cost-justified core and the debt-service allocation) versus finding the district's overall methodology reasonable enough — given the genuine 1.6x cost differential and the art. X §2 conservation mandate — to uphold the surcharge largely intact; this turns on unsettled post-Capistrano case law (including recent decisions like the unverified Coziahr) that I have not independently confirmed."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not that a reviewing court finds at least a substantial part of the Water Resilience Surcharge invalid under art. XIII D, § 6(b)(3), because the district's own rate study admits the $0.85 figure is derived "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation (the core defect condemned in Capistrano), and because a majority of the revenue funds rebates that the district concedes mostly benefit non-payors — a cross-subsidy problem independent of the pricing defect. The district's fallback "not a property-related fee" argument is weak under Bighorn and unlikely to avoid merits review. Countervailing cost-justification (the 1.6x marginal-cost finding) and the conservation mandate/Water Code § 375 give the district real, non-frivolous defenses that could save the debt-service (30%) portion and possibly cap rather than eliminate the surcharge, which is why this isn't a near-certainty.
rationales: Capistrano holds tiered rates must track the actual marginal cost of service at that tier, not conservation goals alone, and the district's concession that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation is close to the precise defect Capistrano invalidated.

The rate study's own 1.6x marginal-cost finding sets an internal benchmark against which the $0.85 surcharge can be tested, so any increment above a cost-justified differential is independently vulnerable even if the whole surcharge is not struck down.

Routing 55% of surcharge revenue to rebates that the district admits 'mostly benefit customers other than those paying the surcharge' creates a cross-subsidization theory distinct from the pricing theory, giving plaintiffs two independent paths to a 6(b)(3) violation and increasing the odds that at least 'a substantial part' falls.

The district's alternative argument that the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all is likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil, which treats usage-based charges to existing water customers as property-related fees, so the case will almost certainly be resolved on the cost-of-service merits rather than dismissed at the threshold.

Countervailing factors — a genuine 1.6x cost differential, Article X § 2's conservation mandate, Water Code § 375's express legislative endorsement of conservation pricing, and Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir's allowance of capital/debt-service cost recovery through volumetric rates (supporting the 30% recycled-water debt-service allocation) — give the district a real chance of preserving a meaningful part of the structure, which is why I place this below 75 rather than higher.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372
Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) — recent appellate treatment of tiered-rate proportionality, holding unverified, flag for confirmation
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court will treat the conceded 'conservation-incentive' pricing component and the rebate cross-subsidy as severable, partial defects (satisfying 'substantial part' invalidation while preserving the cost-justified core and the debt-service allocation) versus finding the district's overall methodology reasonable enough — given the genuine 1.6x cost differential and the art. X §2 conservation mandate — to uphold the surcharge largely intact; this turns on unsettled post-Capistrano case law (including recent decisions like the unverified Coziahr) that I have not independently confirmed.
